segments.s725-g17: برآن گونه = a prepositional/adverbial phrase 'in that way / in such a manner' placed before the verb for emphasis. برگاشتمشان is past-tense 1st-person singular + enclitic object -شان ('I removed/lifted/took them'); ز رزم is the prepositional complement 'from (the) battle' with the poetic preposition ز (ز = از). Word order is cause/manner + verb + object + source.
segments.s725-g18: که introduces a result clause ('so that / such that'). نه before رزم negates 'battle' ('no battle'), and بینند is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive form meaning 'they may see / they would see' used here with a result sense. زان پس = 'thereafter / from that on' (زان = 'from that', پس = 'after'), and نه بزم = 'nor (any) feast/banquet'.
segments.s725-g19: بسی here functions as an intensifier ('much / many'); نامور مهتران is a noun phrase 'renowned chiefs/elders' (مهتران = great ones/chiefs). با منند is a verbless/copular structure meaning 'are with me' (با = with; منند is a present plural form indicating the state 'they are' / 'they stand'), so the whole yields 'many celebrated chiefs are with me.'
segments.s725-g20: نبادی is an optative/jussive negative wish ('may it not be' / 'let it not happen'). که introduces the content of that wish ('that / that...'), آید is 3rd-person singular present/subjunctive of آمدن used here in the sense 'befall / come (to pass)'. بریشان = بر + ایشان 'upon them', and گزند is the object 'harm / injury'. Together: 'may it not be that harm come upon them.'
segments.s725-g21: نشستم is simple past 1st-person 'I sat (down / remained)'. به + آموی marks the locative/benefactive relation 'by/at my uncle' (آموی = paternal uncle). تا here is the subordinating conjunction 'until', and پاسخم is the noun 'my answer/reply' with the enclitic possessive -م 'my'. The segment sets up a temporal expectation: 'I sat by my uncle until my reply...'.
segments.s725-g22: بیارد is a 3rd-person singular subjunctive/jussive of آوردن 'to bring' used elliptically (the object 'پاسخم' is understood from the previous segment): 'until (it) be brought'. مگر introduces an exception 'unless', and اختر فرخم is the noun phrase 'the fortunate/lucky star' (اختر = star; فرخم = fortunate). So: '...until my reply be brought, unless my lucky star brought it.'
segments.s725-g9: نخستین سخن گفت بهرام گرد is an inverted, compact clause: نخســتین سخن ("the first speech/words") is the direct phrase and گفت is the simple past verb "said/spoke." The name بهرام گرد follows the verb phrase as the subject/agent; گرد here is an epithet attached to the name. Word order (object/phrase — verb — subject) is typical of poetic/epic Persian.
segments.s725-g10: به تیمار و درد برادر بمرد gives the cause of death: به marks the cause/relationship ('with/through/for'), تیمار و درد form a coordinated noun phrase 'care and pain/grief', برادر is the genitive object 'for his brother', and بمرد is the (archaic/poetic) past form 'he died.' In epic Persian the prefixal b- appears in some past forms; here بمرد functions as a past-tense verb meaning 'he died (of).' The whole segment reads 'he died from care and sorrow for his brother.'
segments.s725-g11: تو را و مرا مزد بسیار باد is an optative/wish construction. تو را و مرا place the beneficiaries ('for you and for me') with را marking them as the affected parties, مزد بسیار is the noun phrase 'great reward', and باد is the optative particle 'may it be'. The sentence literally: 'May a great reward be to you and to me.'
segments.s725-g12: روان وی از ما بی‌آزار باد is a parallel optative clause: روان وی 'his soul' is the subject, از ما 'from us' gives the source/relationship, بی‌آزار 'free from harm / unharmed' is a predicative adjective, and باد again is the optative 'may it be.' Together: 'May his soul be unharmed because of us.'
segments.s725-g13: دگر گفت با شهریار بلند: دگر here means 'again' (a temporal/adverbial use), گفت is the past 'he said/spoke', با + شهریار marks the indirect object 'to/with the king', and بلند is an epithet 'lofty/exalted' modifying شهریار. The phrase is a simple clause 'again he spoke to the lofty king.'
segments.s725-g14: بگوی آنچ از من شنیدی ز پند is an imperative/report clause: بگوی is the verb 'tell/say' in an addressive/imperative sense, آنچ 'that which/whatever' is the relative object, از من شنیدی 'you heard from me' places the source (شنیدی is 2nd-person past 'you heard'), and ز پند 'concerning/from advice' specifies that what was heard was advice. Word order puts the imperative verb first, then the object and its source phrase.
segments.s725-g15: پس ما بیامد سپاهی گران shows the flexible word order of epic Persian: پس 'then', ما functions as the dative/experiencer 'upon us' or 'to us', بیامد is the past 'came', and سپاهی گران is the subject that follows the verb ('a heavy/formidable army' or 'formidable troops'). The clause thus means 'then a formidable army came against us.'
segments.s725-g16: همه نامداران جنگاوران is a nominative/nominal apposition emphasizing who comprised the army: همه 'all', نامداران 'renowned/illustrious ones', and جنگاوران 'warriors/fighters'. The three words together function as a single noun phrase 'all the renowned warriors.'
segments.s725-g1: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause; پیروز is an adjective 'victorious'; شد is the past of شدن 'became' forming 'became victorious.' سوی ایران is a prepositional phrase 'toward Iran' and کشید (poetic use of کشیدن) is a past tense main verb meaning 'marched/advanced,' placed at the end as common in Persian poetic word order.
segments.s725-g2: بر here means 'against' (indicating direction of action toward an adversary); شهریار is 'king' and دلیران is the plural 'the brave' used after شهریار to mean 'king of the brave' or 'the brave king' (poetic juxtaposition instead of an explicit Ezāfe); کشید is the same past verb 'advanced/moved against.'
segments.s725-g3: به‌روز چهارم is a temporal phrase 'on the fourth day' (به‌روز = 'on the day/of the day'); به introduces the destination 'to' and آموی is 'uncle (paternal)'; شد is a past verb here implying arrival/occurrence ('he came' / 'it happened').
segments.s725-g4: ندیدی is the past negative 'you did not see' (here rhetorical: 'have you not seen'); زنی 'a woman' is the patient/subject of the implied seeing; کو functions as a relative/archaising interrogative 'who/that' introducing the relative clause; جهانجوی is a compound adjective 'world‑seeking' and شد is the past 'became' ('who became a world‑seeker').
segments.s725-g5: به آموی is a locative/directional phrase 'to/with his uncle'; یک چند is an idiomatic temporal expression 'for a short while / a little while'; بنشست is the simple past 'he sat down' (here 'he sat [with him]'); و بود links to the next clause with بود as the past copula 'and he was / remained.'
segments.s725-g6: به دلش اندرون is a doubled locative 'in his inner heart' (به + اندرون = inside, with ش = his); داوریها is the plural subject 'judgments/decisions/thoughts'; فزود is an archaic past form meaning 'increased' or 'multiplied,' so the clause says these judgments grew within him.
segments.s725-g7: یکی is 'a certain' or 'one (certain)'; نامه is 'letter'; سوی برادر is the directional phrase 'to his brother' (suitable for the recipient); بدرد here functions idiomatically as 'for the benefit/use of' (literally 'useful to'), so the phrase describes the intended recipient/benefit of the letter.
segments.s725-g8: نوشت is the simple past 'he wrote'; و 'and' links the next action; ز in this poetic usage means 'about' or 'concerning'; هر کارش 'every/each of his affairs' (هر = every, کار = affair, ش = his); آگاه کرد is a causative phrase 'made (him) aware' = 'informed him.'

sentences.s725-s9: I removed them from the fighting in such a manner that afterward they would see neither battle nor feast.
sentences.s725-s10: Many renowned chiefs are with me; may it not be that any harm come upon them.
sentences.s725-s11: I sat by my uncle until my reply should be brought — unless my fortunate star (i.e. my good fortune) brought it.
sentences.s725-s5: First Bahram Gerd spoke a few words; he died of care and grief for his brother.
sentences.s725-s6: May great reward be yours and mine; may his soul be free from harm because of us.
sentences.s725-s7: Again he spoke to the lofty sovereign: 'Tell him whatever you heard from my counsel.'
sentences.s725-s8: Then a heavy (formidable) army came upon us — all the renowned warriors, the fighters.
sentences.s725-s1: When he became victorious, he marched toward Iran and moved against the king of the brave.
sentences.s725-s2: On the fourth day he went to his uncle; have you not seen a woman who became a seeker of the world?
sentences.s725-s3: He sat with his uncle for a while and remained; inwardly, judgments multiplied in his heart.
sentences.s725-s4: He wrote a certain letter to his brother and informed him of all his affairs.
